food_subgroup: Stir-fried flat rice noodles with seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for dry seafood hor fun. FOODON_03302051 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for stir-fried flat rice noodles with seafood.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual Asian noodle preparations at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: flat rice noodles (hor fun / he fen)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of dry seafood hor fun weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as served in a Singapore or Filipino-Chinese café.

nutrient_source.title: Hor Fun, Stir-fried with Seafood (Dry) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite estimate for stir-fried flat rice noodle dish with mixed seafood
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists hor fun dishes including seafood variants. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for stir-fried flat rice noodles with seafood (dry style), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for individual components (flat rice noodles, prawns, squid, egg, bean sprouts, soy sauce). The dry style (no gravy) results in slightly lower sodium and moisture compared to wet hor fun. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate for stir-fried flat rice noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry seafood hor fun as a composite dish. The GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI approximately 61, medium category), which form the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Stir-frying with protein (seafood, egg) and fat (cooking oil) is expected to modestly attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain boiled rice noodles. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 39.5, rounded to 40.1 accounting for mixed ingredients. Serving carbohydrate = 18.5 g/100 g × 350 g = 64.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g (1,680 mg per standard 350 g serving), dry seafood hor fun contributes a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to half a plate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index rice noodle base with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 61 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 40 per standard serving, dry seafood hor fun can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g), pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from seafood and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate cholesterol (approximately 72 mg per 100 g) from prawns, squid, and egg warrant caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing smaller portions, requesting less sauce, and balancing the meal with fibre-rich vegetables can help mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Seafood Hor Fun (干炒海鲜河粉) is a stir-fried flat rice noodle dish featuring mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish cake — tossed in a hot wok with egg, bean sprouts, spring onion, garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. Unlike the 'wet' version served with a thick egg-based gravy, the dry style is wok-tossed until the noodles are lightly charred and coated in sauce without excess liquid, producing a slightly smoky, savoury flavour with a tender-chewy texture. The dish is rooted in Cantonese Chinese culinary tradition and is widely served across Singapore and the Philippines in Chinese-style cafés and hawker stalls. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (approximately 142 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from seafood and egg (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g), and a medium glycemic index of approximately 61 driven by the flat rice noodle base. Sodium content is high due to soy and oyster sauces, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and provides useful amounts of vitamin B12, iron, and zinc from the seafood components.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'dry seafood hor fun' as a named dish was confirmed at time of processing. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo stir-fried flat rice noodle with seafood data and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice noodles. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in stir-fried dishes using vegetable cooking oil per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. The food is listed under Filipino cuisine in the input (served at Lucky Cafe, a Filipino café on North Canal Road, Singapore); however, hor fun is a Cantonese Chinese dish widely adopted in Filipino-Chinese cuisine. Region of origin is noted accordingly. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain non-halal oyster sauce or be prepared in a non-halal kitchen; this should be verified at the restaurant level. 7. GI glycemic load per serving calculated using 350 g serving size and 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g: (61 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 40.1.
